items.correct: 7 - x — the minus sign outside the bracket changes the sign of every term inside (3 - x + 4).
items.misconception: 3 - x - 4 = -1 - x, by only applying the minus to the first term inside.
items.why: Students treat the leading minus as if it only affects the first term, forgetting that subtracting a bracket changes every sign inside.

items.correct: The square root of x (the positive root when x is positive).
items.misconception: x^(1/2) means x divided by 2 — half of x.
items.random_wrong: x^(1/2) means 1 divided by x squared.
items.why: Fraction-as-division intuition takes over; students don't connect fractional exponents to roots.

items.correct: No — a prime number has exactly two distinct factors (1 and itself); 1 has only one factor.
items.misconception: Yes — 1 has no factors except itself, which makes it prime.
items.random_wrong: Yes — 1 is the smallest prime number.
items.why: Students apply a loose 'only divisible by 1 and itself' rule and miss the precise requirement of exactly two distinct factors.

items.q: Using Newton-Raphson on f(x) = x² − 2 with x₀ = 1.5, what is x₁? (Give to 3 dp.)
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1.417. “1.500” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The trapezium rule estimates which kind of quantity?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: a definite integral. “a derivative” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Use trapezium rule with 2 strips to estimate ∫₀² x² dx (h = 1).
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
